title: Anger at Your Younger Body for Not Knowing
dek: You carry a fierce, unspoken anger at the body you once inhabited - the one that didn’t understand, that flinched, that survived without the wisdom you now claim. This is not a failure of will or spirit, but the biology of a younger self who did the best it could with the tools it had. Here, you learn to listen to that anger as a muscle memory, a message from the masseter to the psoas, and to meet it with the respect and herbal allyship it deserves.
bodyMarkdown:
You sit in the dim light of your bedroom at 3 a.m., the air dense with the sharp scent of night jasmine pressed against your window. Your jaw clenches - masseter tightening with the weight of memories you thought you’d left behind. That familiar ache, the one that crawls up your trapezius and snakes along your levator scapulae, is anger - not at the world, not at those who hurt you, but at your younger body. The one that didn’t know. The one that survived without the tools you now hold in your hands. This anger is raw, unfiltered, and deeply somatic. It is not pathology. It is biology.


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.093Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

When you direct fury at the body you once inhabited, you are touching a lineage of survival embedded in fascia, muscle, and breath. Susan Drury, a working herbalist and somatic guide, invites you to meet this anger without shame. To understand that your younger self, the one who flinched, trembled, or froze, was operating with a nervous system that had not yet learned to articulate its own resilience. The diaphragm’s spasms, the psoas’s guarded contraction were not failures but embodied knowledge. This is a body-first messenger, not a moral failing.

Thich Nhat Hanh, the Vietnamese Zen master who taught the world to breathe with presence, reminds us that "the body knows how to heal when we offer it mindfulness and compassion." When you lay your hand on your sternum and feel the rise and fall of your breath - slow, deliberate, intentional - you begin to hold space for that younger body. You acknowledge the tightness in the masseter, the coil in the psoas, the weighted heaviness in the trapezius as messages, not burdens. This is not about forcing forgiveness or rushing into lightness. It is about the slow, steady uncoiling of muscle memory that has been holding onto unspoken truths.

Your anger is a sacred fire, not an enemy. It burns in the jaw where you once bit down to silence your pain, in the diaphragm where breath caught mid-flight under the weight of fear. Karla McLaren, a leading voice in emotional intelligence, writes extensively about the wisdom of anger as an emotion that "protects boundaries and fuels action." She asserts that anger is not the problem; the problem is how we have been taught to ignore or suppress it. The younger you, who didn’t know how to name or release this anger, carried it in the body like a secret tattoo written in muscle tension and breath holding.

Imagine sitting with that younger body in the quiet of a rainy afternoon. The air smells like wet earth and crushed sage. You place your hands gently on your abdomen, just below the ribcage, where the diaphragm flexes with each breath. As you inhale, you imagine drawing oxygen not just into your lungs but into the tight fibers of your psoas, your levator scapulae, the whole network of muscle and fascia that remembers. As you exhale, you release - not the anger itself, but the idea that it must be hidden or feared.

Here, herbs become allies. Your herbalist roots remind you that nature offers specific medicines to support the nervous system and soothe muscular tension. Skullcap, for instance, is a nervine that eases the diaphragm’s spasms and calms the tremor in the masseter. Valerian root, steeped as a dark, earthy tea, invites your body into deeper rest, encouraging the psoas to soften and release its guarded contraction. These are not quick fixes but companions in the slow work of listening and responding without judgment.

The night is thick with the scent of mugwort burning on your windowsill, a traditional herb used to guard dreams and open inner pathways. As smoke curls around your shoulders, you recall Robert Augustus Masters’ teachings on somatic psychotherapy. Masters emphasizes that trauma and its associated anger are stored in the body’s tissues, that the jaw remembers what the mind has forgotten. "Trauma is held in the body," he says, "and the pathway to healing runs through embodied presence." You feel this presence as a tremor in the diaphragm, a pulse in the trapezius, a warm ache that is not pain but recognition.

You begin a simple embodied practice. Inhale slowly through the nose for a count of four, letting the breath move deep into the belly. Hold for a count of two. Exhale through the mouth for a count of six, feeling the ribs soften, the neck release tension, the masseter unclench. Repeat this cycle five times, each breath inviting the body to remember that it is safe to let go. This is not about erasing anger but about befriending it, walking alongside it like an old friend who has carried your story.

The younger body’s anger is often accompanied by a scent - a sharpness in the sweat on your skin, a metallic tang in your mouth, a trace of earthiness that clings to your hair after a storm. It’s the body’s language, a vocabulary of sensation that demands attention. You do not dismiss this language. Instead, you lean into it, honoring the biology of anger as a messenger. Gabor Maté, who bridges trauma and addiction, reminds us that "the body’s response is always an attempt to protect itself." The tension in your levator scapulae, the flutter in your diaphragm, the tightening around your masseter are protective responses from a younger self who had to survive.

You may find, over time, that this anger transforms. It does not vanish but shifts from a clenched fist in the jaw to a steady pulse in the heart. It becomes a wellspring of resilience, a signal that you are fiercely alive and fiercely protective of your boundaries. Resmaa Menakem, a somatic therapist focused on racialized trauma, teaches that "healing happens in the body first." You feel this truth in the slow unwinding of your psoas, the gentle release of your trapezius, the softening of your breath.

As you move through your day, you carry this embodied knowledge with you. The younger body’s anger is no longer a weight but a teacher. You might light a cup of chamomile tea, letting its gentle floral bitterness soothe the nervous system. Or you might press a warm compress of lavender and peppermint to your neck, inviting the levator scapulae to relax. Each act is a ceremony, a recognition that your body holds wisdom beyond words.

In those moments when anger rises suddenly - when you catch yourself clenching your jaw at the office or feeling the tight coil in your psoas during a tense conversation - you return to your breath. You place your hand over your heart, feeling the steady thump beneath your palm. You whisper gratitude to your younger self for surviving, for carrying the anger so you don’t have to carry it alone anymore. This is not a lightness born of denial but a grounded presence born of deep, somatic understanding.

The work is ongoing. There will be nights when the masseter clenches so hard you can taste blood, when the psoas knots so tightly you feel trapped in your own body. In those moments, you reach for your allies - herbs, breath, presence, and the teachings of those who have walked this path before. You remember Pema Chödrön’s words on discomfort: "The most fundamental aggression to ourselves, the most fundamental harm we can do to ourselves, is to remain ignorant by not having the courage and the respect to look at ourselves honestly and gently."

Your anger at your younger body is a call to that courage and respect. It is a fierce, unyielding love that refuses to let your story be erased. It is the biology of survival speaking through the tissues, the muscles, the breath. This is not a wound to be hidden but a map to be read, a language to be learned. The body knows. The jaw remembers. You are here to listen.

**FAQ**

**Q: Why do I feel anger directed at my younger body instead of at the people who hurt me?**

**A:** Anger directed at your younger body often arises because that part of you was the first to experience trauma or neglect without the tools to process it. This anger is an expression of internalized survival mechanisms - a way your nervous system holds onto protection. It’s not about blaming yourself but recognizing that the body stores these experiences and their emotional weight.

**Q: How can herbs really help with somatic anger?**

**A:** Herbs like skullcap, valerian, and chamomile have long histories as nervines - plants that specifically support the nervous system by calming muscle tension and reducing anxiety. When used thoughtfully, they can ease the physical symptoms of anger, such as diaphragm spasms or jaw clenching, making it easier to engage with the underlying emotional messages without overwhelm.

**Q: What if I don’t feel comfortable sitting with this anger?**

**A:** It’s normal to feel resistance. Anger held in the body can be intense and unfamiliar if you’ve been taught to suppress it. Start with small, gentle embodied practices - like breathwork or gentle movement - and seek support from somatic guides or therapists who honor the body’s wisdom. Remember that this is a gradual process of building trust with your body.

**Q: How do I know if my anger is healthy or if it’s becoming destructive?**

**A:** Healthy anger serves as a protective signal and a motivator for setting boundaries. It feels contained within you and can be expressed in ways that do not harm yourself or others. If anger feels overwhelming, uncontrollable, or leads to harmful actions, it’s important to seek professional support. Somatic therapies can help integrate these intense emotions safely.

**Q: Can the teachings of Thich Nhat Hanh and Karla McLaren be combined in my practice?**

**A:** Absolutely. Thich Nhat Hanh’s emphasis on mindful breathing and compassionate presence complements Karla McLaren’s work on emotional intelligence and boundary-setting. Together, they offer a powerful framework for honoring anger as a messenger, grounding it in the body, and responding with both awareness and action.

This work is not easy, and it is not quick. But as you hold your younger body with fierce tenderness, you transform anger from a clenched wound into a living, breathing messenger. The body remembers. You are learning to listen.

---

<section data-author="byline">
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.
Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.093Z">May 28, 2026</time>.
In our work on anger at your younger body for not knowing, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
</section>
